questions.question: What is the 'Presentation Trap' (or 'Premature Offer Trap')?

options.text: Using a slide deck that is too long
Showing features before understanding the prospect's desired outcomes
Forgetting to share your screen on Zoom
Charging too little for your product

questions.explanation: The Presentation Trap occurs when founders focus on 'how' the product works (features) rather than the 'destination' (business outcomes). This forces the prospect to do the hard work of translating tech into value.

questions.question: According to the lesson, what is a 'Feature Dump' comparable to in the technical world?

options.text: A clean, intuitive UI
Handing a non-technical user a full Swagger/API documentation
A well-written README file
A bug-free production release

questions.explanation: A feature dump is technically accurate but practically useless to a buyer who just wants to solve a problem. It provides too much 'under-the-hood' detail without enough context on ROI.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of the 'So What?' Filter?

options.text: To find bugs in your code
To ensure every feature you show maps directly to a business outcome or emotional benefit
To check if your product is compatible with other software
To make the demo longer and more impressive

questions.explanation: If a feature doesn't have a strong 'So What?' (a reason the prospect should care about their own time/money), it should be removed from the demo to maintain focus on value.

questions.question: What is the main difference between a 'Tour Guide' and a 'Value Architect'?

options.text: The Tour Guide has a better camera
The Value Architect focuses on the 'Compelling Event' and skips irrelevant features
The Tour Guide shows more technical architecture
There is no difference; they are both sales roles

questions.explanation: The Tour Guide follows a rigid, linear path through the product. The Value Architect tailors the demo to solve the specific 'pain' uncovered in discovery, value-mapping the experience.

questions.question: Why should the feature with the highest 'Emotional Impact' be showed first?

options.text: Because it's usually the most expensive
To hook the prospect's attention immediately and prove the 'Why' before the 'How'
To get the hardest part of the demo out of the way
Because it's the newest feature in the product

questions.explanation: Showing the 'Wow' moment first earns you the right to show the necessary but 'boring' parts of the product later. It anchors the demo in high value from the start.
